issues.title: [IOS]TextField throws assertion error on long press
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. Enter some text into the TextField
2. Long press on the text

### Expected results

No exception is thrown and a magnifier is shown.

### Actual results

Exception is constantly thrown and magnifier does not appear.

### Code sample

<details open><summary>Code sample</summary>

```dart
import 'package:flutter/material.dart';

void main() {
  runApp(MyApp());
}

class MyApp extends StatelessWidget {
  const MyApp({super.key});

  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return MaterialApp(
      home: Scaffold(body: SafeArea(child: Center(child: TextInput()))),
    );
  }
}

class TextInput extends StatefulWidget {
  const TextInput({super.key});

  @override
  State<TextInput> createState() => _TextInputState();
}

class _TextInputState extends State<TextInput> {
  @override
  Widget build(BuildContext context) {
    return Container(
      height: 56,
      padding: const EdgeInsets.symmetric(horizontal: 20, vertical: 8),
      decoration: BoxDecoration(
        border: Border.all(color: Colors.black, width: 1),
      ),
      child: TextField(
        decoration: InputDecoration(
          contentPadding: const EdgeInsets.only(bottom: 8),
          label: Text('input'),
        ),
      ),
    );
  }
}

```

</details>

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/f9f6953ef7467966852bea255111ebab0020ac6e/packages/flutter/lib/src/rendering/editable.dart#L2474-L2477

I am not sure why `bottomBound` is calculated like it is, but it ends up being negative in this situation and fails the assert. 

Updating this to always use the `_textPainter.height` fixed it, but I am not sure if anything else is broken now.
```dart
final double bottomBound =
        _textPainter.height -
        preferredLineHeight +
        floatingCursorAddedMargin.bottom;
```

Container(height:34,child:TextField(..))

@justinmc the behavior in this case changed with https://github.com/flutter/flutter/commit/6d8f5399712a1597edae61644922de0cd22ee834.  Is this working as intended?

I was using material TextField, I switched to Cupertino one, and it works.
But I think 6d8f539 will not fix material TextField
I found that it is a height problem. If you set the height to 42, you will not have this problem. How to set the height of the textField?

~~This appears to be fixed in the latest master thanks to @Piinks and https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/55408 🎉~~

~~Let me know if anyone can still see this on master.~~

I was too quick in my testing, looks like it still happens on my Pixel 2.
I use the stable branch and look forward to pushing it to the stable branch

Here's a workaround that seems to fix it for me: use `isDense`.

```dart
          Container(
            height: 34,
            child: TextField(
              maxLines: 1,
              decoration: InputDecoration(
                isDense: true,
              ),
            ),
          ),
```

I spent some time looking into this and couldn't get a fix yet, but I do see that it's affected by the padding used in [InputDecorator's _layout](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/fdc4d21b79034b96aadba6a1e9cc273178073aee/packages/flutter/lib/src/material/input_decorator.dart#L951).
Using the `InputDecorator.contentPadding` seems to have some effect on it. But that's not a fix.

I just had the same problem to solve it I set the following properties: 
maxLines:1, contentPadding: EdgeInsets.zero

The issue seems to be caused by `contentPadding`. Is there a way to fix the bottom text clipping without lowering the contentPadding's values by changing the values of `contentPadding`, `isDense`, or `isCollpasible`? I have a hunch that the alloted height for `Text` is incorrect when it contains the letters `p, q, g, y, etc.`

Yes, `contentPadding` is closely involved. Specifically what's happening is that the text field is overflowing vertically — it's been given a height that isn't enough to fit the text and all the decoration.

And currently that overflow seems to work until it suddenly becomes a problem, as @bleroux explained [on another thread](https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/110254#issuecomment-1249188457):
> One side of [this issue] is that the `RenderEditable` draws outside of its reported bounds while the text is short enough. This leads to the text suddenly disappearing only when it overflows (clipping is added only when the text overflows).

When you're seeing this in an app, the root problem is that you're trying to use a Material Design TextField, but at the same time trying to specify a height that's shorter than Material Design calls for. So the TextField goes ahead and tries to claim for the decoration all the padding that the decoration expects, and that doesn't leave enough height for the text.

To make the field shorter without causing this problem, instead of setting a short `height`, you should adjust the `InputDecoration` by setting `isDense`, or `contentPadding`, or both. Here's an example:
https://github.com/zulip/zulip-flutter/blob/cd4ff5e3231475f3a4ff6a81b1f0fdc6c9394aaf/lib/widgets/compose_box.dart#L277-L281
```dart
      inputDecorationTheme: InputDecorationTheme(
        // Both [contentPadding] and [isDense] combine to make the layout compact.
        isDense: true,
        contentPadding: const EdgeInsets.symmetric(
            horizontal: 12.0, vertical: 8.0),
```

---

Meanwhile, the issue to be fixed in the framework here is that there should be a warning when the text field is overflowing vertically, so that it doesn't sneak up on you by having clipping suddenly take effect when the text gets long. That's the plan discussed above e.g. at https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/51257#issuecomment-673712875 , but it hasn't yet been implemented. (An initial implementation #63639 got reverted, as #64573.)

Thanks for your insights @gnprice .

> To make the field shorter without causing this problem, instead of setting a short `height`, you should adjust the `InputDecoration` by setting `isDense`, or `contentPadding`, or both.

This issue also occurs without setting a height. Just setting the border to `OutlineInputBorder` also causes the issue:
```dart
InputDecoration(
  border: const OutlineInputBorder(),
  enabledBorder: OutlineInputBorder(
      borderSide: BorderSide(color: Colors.red),
   ),
),
```

issues.title: CJK word boundaries
comments:
Similar remarks apply to Japanese. I think probably any solution we find for Chinese will come with a solution for Japanese and vice versa, so there isn't much separate work to do for one vs. the other; mentioning it here mainly so the issue matches the term "japanese" in searches.

Here's some example Japanese text, again from [a Wikipedia article](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/語):

> たとえば日本語の辞典で、「考えた」「考えない」「考えれば」「考えろ」などとは記載されず、「考える」だけが記載される。

I just tried the repro steps and confirmed they give the same results on that text as for Chinese.

This issue is reproducible on the latest `stable` and `master` channels. I also see there are different behaviors between MacOS apps:

### Native apps
- Notes app on MacOS: the cursor seems to be moved by word (I'm not very familiar with Chinese text)
`1894` `年` `，孫文` `成立` `興中會` `。1895` `年`
- Android Studio IDE: the cursor moves until it reaches punctuation. (similar to `gedit` on Linux in the demo at https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/106472#issuecomment-1399082708)


### Flutter apps
- Web: has the same behavior as _Notes app on MacOS_ above ✅

Typically Japanese, Chinese, Thai, Khmer, Lao and Myanmar/Burmese would require dictionary-based word breaking algo and indeed the data included in ICU is rather large.
https://unicode-org.github.io/icu/userguide/boundaryanalysis/

I guess a simplistic approach to have something to start with but w/o dict, could involve:
1) stop at all spaces, including ZWSP
2) stop at all punctuation marks
3) stop at numbers
Maybe to cover all 1-3 stopping at all characters whose script Unicode property is different from the current one could be a good start.

FWIW, Flutter Web engine has a good word breaker implementing Unicode rules (naturally w/o dict): https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/main/lib/web_ui/lib/src/engine/text/word_breaker.dart

BTW (to get an idea of sizes), just for Lao there's this old project:
https://code.google.com/archive/p/lao-dictionary/source/default/source

> I guess a simplistic approach to have something to start with but w/o dict, could involve:

This sounds similar to the gedit behavior described at https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/106472#issuecomment-1399082708 .

To copy into this thread what I said there: that behavior isn't great (it means moving past entire long clauses)… but it's still probably worthwhile to make Ctrl+Left/Right do that, instead of one character at a time, if we think we won't be able to do real word boundaries anytime soon. After all, if you want a character at a time you can always use plain left/right without Ctrl.


> Maybe to cover all 1-3 stopping at all characters whose script Unicode property is different from the current one could be a good start.

Hmm, will that do it? I'd have thought that punctuation like 。 and 、 that are used in Japanese, for example, would be in the same script as Japanese text.

> BTW (to get an idea of sizes), just for Lao there's this old project:
> https://code.google.com/archive/p/lao-dictionary/source/default/source

Cool. Just to put the numbers explicitly in the thread: that Lao dictionary is
205kB uncompressed
46kB compressed in that zip archive
36kB compressed with `xz`

> Hmm, will that do it? I'd have thought that punctuation like 。 and 、 that are used in Japanese, for example, would be in the same script as Japanese text.

Good question there. Just checked in Unicode data (https://unicode.org/Public/UNIDATA/UnicodeData.txt and https://unicode.org/Public/UNIDATA/Scripts.txt):

- "。" is `3002;IDEOGRAPHIC FULL STOP;Po;0;ON;;;;;N;IDEOGRAPHIC PERIOD;;;;`
- it's assigned the "Common" script property: `3001..3003    ; Common # Po   [3] IDEOGRAPHIC COMMA..DITTO MARK`

Looks like that does a good job on the languages that use spaces and don't require dicts. Is improvement needed there? At a quick test, it seems like Ctrl+Left/Right are already doing fine for those, modulo #78660. (I tried the examples in Greek, Devanagari, and Arabic script.)

Well, the test code output is from the `WordBreaker` that's in the engine's web_ui which is a complete Unicode implementation (https://unicode.org/reports/tr29/#Word_Boundary_Rules). What we have in the framework (`WordBoundary`) does not implement all the rules (it doesn't use Unicode char properties). So this could be the first improvement.

The second improvement, for Chinese/Han and Japanese/Kanji, could be to use the Unicode script properties to stop on punctuation. This would be non-standard (while no dicts are available) and I guess care should be taken not to break Unicode rules such as https://unicode.org/reports/tr29/#WB11).

> This would be non-standard (while no dicts are available) and I guess care should be taken not to break Unicode rules such as https://unicode.org/reports/tr29/#WB11).

Hmm, I see. Looking at that table, perhaps we can implement the "script property breaking" idea in the form of inserting additional rules into the list? It looks like that's the intended way for an implementation to tailor the behavior.

For example, looking in the CLDR tree, here's what they have [in common/segments/ja.xml](https://github.com/unicode-org/cldr/blob/8f04544c6f31812a6bfd2fecc6ca6d2fd715771a/common/segments/ja.xml) for customizing word breaks for Japanese:
```xml
		<segmentation type="WordBreak">
			<variables>
				<variable id="$Hiragana">\p{Hiragana}</variable>
				<variable id="$Hiragana">($Hiragana $FE*)</variable>
				<variable id="$Ideographic">[[\p{Ideographic}] [\u3005 \u3007 \u303B]]</variable>
				<variable id="$Ideographic">($Ideographic $FE*)</variable>
			</variables>
			<segmentRules>
				<rule id="13.3"> $Hiragana × $Hiragana </rule>
				<rule id="13.4"> $Ideographic × $Ideographic </rule>
			</segmentRules>
		</segmentation>
```

In other words, one follows all the rules in the default table, except that before you reach the rule at the very end which inserts a break if no other rule applies, you forbid breaks within runs of hiragana and runs of "ideographic", where each of those has a definition expressed in the `<variables>` section. (The default rules already forbid breaks within runs of katakana.)

If we take those rules and then add a similar one for each of Thai script, Lao script, Khmer script, and Myanmar/Burmese script, that may do it for as good as we can get short of dictionary data.

Sounds good - the properties missing in UNIDATA/auxiliary/WordBreakProperty.txt seem to be coming from different data though. Ideographic would be UNIDATA/PropList.txt and Hiragana from UNIDATA/Scripts.txt I guess?

South-East asian scripts also have peculiarities;
- Thai and Lao don't use punctuation (space is used as full stop), except maybe for modern texts where you can see Latin punctuation marks: "!", "?", parentheses etc
- Khmer has periods "។" / "៕", colon "៖" and a bunch of old (rarely used?) symbols
- Burmese has diacritics similar to other Brahmic scripts: "၊", "။" plus a few others

I think it would be possible to move word_breakder.dart somewhere that's accessible by both the framework and the engine (like how dart.ui is used in both).  But even that might be a non-trivial size increase for non-web apps?

CC @yjbanov or anyone else more familiar with web that might have some thoughts.

The packed lookup data isn't big really. I can see it's recently been moved to https://github.com/flutter/engine/blob/main/third_party/web_unicode/lib/web_unicode/codegen/word_break_properties.dart

It's 8245 characters so I guess it'd occupy twice that in bytes.
We've recently adopted a new version of ICU data tables for desktop, so proper handling of this will probably be more feasible now.
@gspencergoog How can we determine if those new data tables would properly break up chinese words/other languages? And would they be usable on mobile platforms?

I actually don't know, sorry, just that the tables are now more comprehensive (I was typing the comment for folks at the Desktop Triage meeting)  On mobile the issue would be compilation size, even if it helps break them up properly.
The `triaged-desktop` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-desktop` label or `fyi-desktop` label.
I think this is related to https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/59284, which is about word breaking in Korean.

On mobile the same problem happens in the context of selectable text. When selecting a word by long press or double tap, a single (Japanese) character is selected instead.

Isn't there a workaround for this? Even if I can provide the correct word splitting (see mecab_dart, ve_dart) I can't manage to solve how to select that word between provided boundaries.

issues.title: Action.overridable cannot be overridden by a DoNothingAction
issues.body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. Wrap a `TextField` with `Actions` and use `DoNothingAction` for all text editing `Intent`s (see code sample)
2. Run it, type in some text.
3. Do  some text editing related shortcuts like `arrow left`, `arrow up`, `ctrl + A`, `backspace` etc.

### Expected results

1. `arrow up`, `arrow down`, `page up`, `page down`, `home` and `end` or in other words `ExtendSelectionVerticallyToAdjacentPageIntent` and `ExtendSelectionVerticallyToAdjacentLineIntent` do nothing
2. for all other intents no assertion errors is thrown

### Actual results

1. `ExtendSelectionVerticallyToAdjacentPageIntent` and `ExtendSelectionVerticallyToAdjacentLineIntent` moves caret to the start/end of the text
2. When all other intents are executed, the following assertion error is thrown (example for `SelectAllTextIntent`):

Failed assertion: line 926 pos 9: 'false'

Either the assertion indicates an error in the framework itself, or we should provide substantially more information in this error message to help you determine and fix the underlying cause.

issues.title: `RawKeyEvent` and `RawKeyboard`, et al should be deprecated and removed
issues.body:
## Description

Several years ago, we embarked on a project to improve the keyboard key handling for Flutter.  The new system has been in production use for a while now (at least a couple of years).

However, the legacy interfaces are still present, and on the framework side, this makes for a confusing story for our users, and complicates the code. On the engine side, we are still producing and handling both kinds of keyboard events, which complicates the code and is less efficient.

This document describes the steps that need to be taken in order to complete the deprecation and removal of the old “Raw” APIs and code.

The migration guide for this work has been [published](https://docs.flutter.dev/release/breaking-changes/key-event-migration).

## Affected APIs

The APIs that need to be deprecated and eventually removed are:

- [x] `RawKeyboard`
- [x] `RawKeyEvent`
- [x] `RawKeyDownEvent`
- [x] `RawKeyUpEvent`
- [x] `RawKeyEventData`
- [x] `RawKeyEventDataAndroid`
- [x] `RawKeyEventDataLinux`
- [x] `RawKeyEventDataIos`
- [x] `RawKeyEventDataMacOs`
- [x] `RawKeyEventDataWindows`
- [x] `RawKeyEventDataFuchsia`
- [x] `RawKeyEventDataWeb`
- [x] `RawKeyEventHandler`
- [x] `FocusOnKeyCallback`
- [x] `RawKeyboardListener`
- [x] `KeyEventManager.handleRawKeyMessage`
- [x] `KeyboardSide`
- [x] `ModifierKey`
- [x] `KeyDataTransitMode`
- [x] `KeySimulatorTransitModeVariant`
- [x] `Focus.onKey`
- [x] `FocusScope.onKey`
- [x] `FocusNode.onKey`
- [x] `FocusNode.attach` `onKey` argument.
- [x] `FocusScopeNode.onKey`
- [x] `debugKeyEventSimulatorTransitModeOverride`

Things that still need to be migrated to use only `KeyEvent` instead of `RawKeyEvent` (in some cases these currently work with both, and will eventually only need to work with KeyEvent):

- [x] `ShortcutActivator.accepts`
- [x] `ShortcutActivator.isActivatedBy`
- [x] `ShortcutManager.handleKeypress`
- [ ] `simulateKeyDownEvent`
- [ ] `simulateKeyUpEvent`
- [ ] `KeyEventSimulator`
- [ ] `_HighlightModeManager`
- [ ] _Many tests_

Functionality that existed in `RawKeyEvent` that no longer exists after switch to KeyEvent:

- [x] Convenience functions for determining if a key is pressed need to be implemented on `HardwareKeyboard` to make migration easier
	- `bool isKeyPressed(LogicalKeyboardKey key)`
	- `bool get isControlPressed`
	- `bool get isShiftPressed`
	- `bool get isAltPressed`
	- `bool get isMetaPressed`

Things that need to be removed eventually:

- [ ] All related private APIs
- [ ] Key code generation that refers to `RawKeyEvent`
- [ ] Engine side code that generates `RawKeyEvent` events.

Documentation that needs updating:

- [ ] Documentation references to all deprecated APIs in API docs.
- [ ] Documentation references to all deprecated APIs in website docs.
- [x] Migration guide for the deprecated APIs. (https://github.com/flutter/website/pull/9889)

## Deprecation Timeline

Q4 2023: Deprecate all the API listed above as deprecated.
Q4 2024: Remove `RawKeyboard` and `RawKeyEvent`, et al. from the Flutter Framework.
Q4 2024: Remove related code from the Flutter Engine.
comments:
For the migration away from `RawKeyEvent`, we need to make `ShortcutActivator.accepts` handle `KeyEvent` events. The options appear to be:

1) add a new `acceptsKeyEvent` method that takes a `KeyEvent`, and make people override it (it will break all subclasses when it is added), or 

2) create a sealed base class that only `RawKeyEvent` and `KeyEvent` derive from (`GenericKeyEvent` perhaps) and make the `ShortcutActivator.accepts` method take a `covariant GenericKeyEvent`, and then provide two implementations conditional on which type was given. Later, both `RawKeyEvent` and `GenericKeyEvent` would be removed.

The up side of the first method is that I don't need to relate the two key event classes, but the down side is a huge breaking change up front when the new method is added.

The up side of the second way is that we don't need to rename the method, and we don't break people's subclasses, but the down side is that we'll break all the subclasses anyhow when we remove `GenericKeyEvent`, or at runtime when we switch to sending only `KeyEvent`s.

We could just leave `GenericKeyEvent` in place after removing the deprecated `RawKeyEvent`, but it would have no members, and only one subclass (`KeyEvent`), so it's really pretty useless. We could start its deprecation period when we remove `RawKeyEvent`, I guess, and then remove it two years from now...

The other part of the problem is when to switch over from sending `RawKeyEvent` instances to `ShortcutActivator.accepts` to sending `KeyEvent` instances.  If I go with the sealed base class path, then it will just break a lot of code at runtime, and static failures are preferable to runtime failures, of course.

Here's what I might try:

1) Introduce the sealed base class (`GenericKeyEvent`) and update `ShortcutActivator.accepts`:
	- Introduce the sealed base class `GenericKeyEvent` (which will mean moving implementation of RawKeyEvent in the framework into the same file as `KeyEvent`, but it'll be deprecated and removed eventually).
	- Update the `ShortcutActivator.accepts` method to accept a `covariant GenericKeyEvent.`
	- Maintain the existing implementation of `ShortcutActivator.accepts` for `RawKeyEvent`.
	- Add a new implementation for `ShortcutActivator.accepts` that handles `KeyEvent` objects.

2) Deprecate `RawKeyEvent`:
	- Mark the `RawKeyEvent` class as deprecated.
	- Provide a migration guide for developers to switch from `RawKeyEvent` to `KeyEvent`.
 
3) Transition to `KeyEvent`:
	- Update the code that sends key events to `ShortcutActivator.accepts` to use KeyEvent instead of `RawKeyEvent`.
	- Maintain the existing implementation for `RawKeyEvent` in `ShortcutActivator.accepts` for a deprecation period.

4) Remove `RawKeyEvent`:
	- After the deprecation period, remove the `RawKeyEvent` class.
	- Remove the `RawKeyEvent`-specific implementation from `ShortcutActivator.accepts`.

After talking with Michael, it seems like method 1, above might be the best way to go.  Yes, it causes a breaking change for people who subclass `ShortcutActivator` directly, but most people don't do that.

I'm going to give that a try.

Looks like this change will require a lot of rework in several repos. Been using things like `RawKeyboardListener` and e.g. `RawKeyEventDataMacOs`, `RawKeyEventDataIos`, since that was pretty much the only way to get what was needed done, when it was written **many** years ago.

I've seen there are some newer ways, but no real idea what they are. Yes these old APIs are fairly low level, but once setup they worked very well, so there was no need to fix them or look into the newer APIs, since they were not broken.

I hope good and very in-depth guides on how to replace all the deprecated APIs above will be provided. At least I have no clue where to even begin to look for replacements.

I'm all in for this change, except for the fact that shortcut is super painful. I hate it. Isn't there a better way? I understand its benefits, but the whole intents mixed with commands that are basically static (actions, bindings) make everything so hard. Could you use shortcuts to, say, control a character with WASD, while having everything in the same function? I miss a dynamic approach. Could you swap the behavior depending on user locale? I don't think so. Things are way too limited for my taste, which is the reason I usually don't use shortcuts in my apps.

@bernaferrari This change doesn't really affect shortcuts much, except for the type of the arguments to the `accepts` method.

Have you tried the `CallbackShortcuts` widget instead of `Shortcuts`? It might be what you're looking for. You also might look at `VoidCallbackIntent` if you still want to use `Shortcuts`.

Thanks @gspencergoog for the pointers. I'm sure it will be simple enough once I have a closer look at our usage of the impacted APIs and their replacements. The deprecation policy usually means there is plenty of time (typically about a year) to remove and change deprecated APIs after they have been marked for deprecation in the frameworks. This is typically more than enough for code that is still maintained 😄 🙏🏻 

Despite that, since I happened to see this issue, I can now hopefully get them done before the deprecation even lands in **stable** and test on **master** when I see the PR here merged on master, to verify I got them all.

Have not been using master so actively lately, but I'll get on it soon again. Been grinding away on stable for some production projects. Generally, I think Flutter has finally reached a level where I find fewer reasons to use master or even faster beta cycle. This is good, but of course it means I do not always stay up to date on the latest coming changes.

>  The deprecation policy usually means there is plenty of time (typically about a year) to remove and change deprecated APIs after they have been marked for deprecation in the frameworks.

Yes, there will be at least a year between the time we deprecate and the time we remove the APIs, per our usual deprecation policy.

Okay, with the commit of #136677, the deprecations are in, starting the one year countdown to their removal.

As mentioned in the issue description above, the migration guide for this is here: https://docs.flutter.dev/release/breaking-changes/key-event-migration

We recently migrated our keyboard shortcut listener's `FocusNode` to use `onKeyEvent` instead of `onKey`. Currently, using `tester.sendKeyUpEvent` does not appear to be triggering our handler. Using `tester.sendKeyDownEvent` does work as expected. Is this to be expected until the final checklist items re: simulated events is completed? Thanks in advance, just want to document test failures if we anticipate them passing as-is when this issue is completed.

  0x8A: 0x0079, // Keyboard Int'l 4 前候補 変換 (次候補) 全候補 (Henkan)
  0x8B: 0x007B, // Keyboard Int'l 5 無変換 (Muhenkan)
  0x8C: 0x005C, // Keyboard Int'l 6 (PC9800 Keypad , )
  0x90: 0x00F2, // Keyboard Lang 1 한/영 (Hanguel/English)
  0x91: 0x00F1, // Keyboard Lang 2 한자 (Hanja)
  0x92: 0x0078, // Keyboard Lang 3 かたかな (Katakana)
  0x93: 0x0077, // Keyboard Lang 4 ひらがな (Hiragana)
  0x94: 0x0076, // Keyboard Lang 5 半角/全角 (Zenkaku/Hankaku)
  0xE0: 0x001D, // Left Control
  0xE1: 0x002A, // Left Shift
  0xE2: 0x0038, // Left Alt
  0xE3: 0xE05B, // Left GUI
  0xE4: 0xE01D, // Right Control
  0xE5: 0x0036, // Right Shift
  0xE6: 0xE038, // Right Alt
  0xE7: 0xE05C, // Right GUI
};
```

> Is this not possible to do in the new API?

Correct, this isn't possible in the new API, since it's more platform independent. If detecting the layout is something that makes sense in a cross platform way, we can work on figuring out a way to represent that.

> Correct, this isn't possible in the new API, since it's more platform independent. If detecting the layout is something that makes sense in a cross platform way, we can work on figuring out a way to represent that.

I can't come up with another use-case than ours (OS installer), or possibly if you have like a front-end for OS keyboard settings, so it's probably not worth spending time on unless others see the need for it. We'll be fine with the manual mapping. :)

@deskangel Unfortunately, you will have to rewrite your logic, since the modifier mask is no longer provided.  There are, however, some helper functions that will help you do it: Look at [`HardwareKeyboard.isMetaPressed`](https://main-api.flutter.dev/flutter/services/HardwareKeyboard/isMetaPressed.html), etc.

This is what I am concerned about. This isn't the first time I've had to
rewrite all the codes related to the keyboard handling without seeing any
benefits. Are the breaking changes caused by the removal absolutely
necessary, or is it just for the sake of tidiness or clean?

It is necessary in order to reduce the maintenance cost of maintaining the system, and to reduce the complexity of the system for stability and quality. It's not just because we want it "tidy", it's because we want it to be well engineered and stable, without being overly complex to understand.

I just noticed that HardwareKeyboard.instance.isShiftPressed doesn't work on Android, whereas the RawKeyEvent event.isShiftPressed did work. This applies both to hardware and virtual keyboards. Is this the intended behaviour to drop support for isShiftPressed on Android or should a file a bug for this? I used it to distinguish Enter and Shift+Enter for some cases.

@konrad321 No, that's not intended behavior, although it is intended that it doesn't work with virtual keyboards (because they don't generally generate events on Android). I don't think that ever worked for virtual keyboards.  Could you please file a separate issue for that with details?

@gspencergoog I tested the behaviour again with the Android Emulator and you're right, it didn't worked there even with the old event system. Neither with a hardware keyboard, nor with the virtual keyboard. It seems I have a very special Android device here, that triggers the shift key as an event. Since this behaviour seems to be device specific and uncommon, I won't file a bug report.
The `triaged-desktop` label is irrelevant if there is no `team-desktop` label or `fyi-desktop` label.

There is also a macOS issue with the new `HardwareKeyboard` which I reported in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/148936 many months ago. I'm also stuck with the old `RawKeyboardListener` until it is resolved.

issues.title: LogicalKeyboardKey does not always match PhysicalKeyboardKey on Desktop(Windows)
issues.body: Trying to grab scanner input, but key.logicalKey.keyLabel sometimes mismatch with physical, as if it still thinks the shift has been pressed. Sometimes there are % insted of 5, and randomly other presented numbers. So that forced me to use code like this, with substring physical key, instead simple key.logicalKey.keyLabel getter. ```void _keyEvent(RawKeyEvent key) { if (key.runtimeType.toString() == 'RawKeyDownEvent' && key.physicalKey.debugName != null) { (_keyTime.isRunning) ? _keyTime.reset() : _keyTime.start(); if (_keyTime.elapsedMilliseconds < _barTime) { var keyname = key.physicalKey.debugName ?? '0'; _barCode += keyname.substring(keyname.length - 1); if (_barTimer == null) { _barTimer = Timer( Duration(milliseconds: _barTime), () => _barProcessor(_barCode)); } else { _barTimer!.cancel(); _barTimer = Timer( Duration(milliseconds: _barTime), () => _barProcessor(_barCode)); } } } } void _barProcessor(String barcode) { var userId = RegExp(r'\d{5,}').firstMatch(barcode)?.group(0) ?? ''; if (userId.isNotEmpty) { print("userId=$userId"); // ... } _keyTime.stop(); _barCode = ''; }

Issue is assigned to multiple teams (design, framework). Please ensure the issue has only one `team-*` label at a time. Use `fyi-*` labels to have another team look at the issue without reassigning it.

I tried to repro this on an android device with an external keyboard following the steps provided but it worked fine on a real device it seems.

I was able to reproduce this in a simulator. When it happens the framework isn't sending any text input related messages and it says "D/InsetsController( 5770): show(ime(), fromIme=true)" in the log. Could someone from the Android team take a look since I'm not familiar with Android debugging?
We where able to reproduce this with an external keyboard.
/cc @reidbaker
The `fyi-android` label is redundant with the `team-android` label.

Note from android triage: We've marked this as a p2 because it does not align with our q4 okrs, and won't be able to get to it this quarter. @LongCatIsLooong would you be interested in pairing with someone on the Android team to look in to this (or another text input related issue)?

Interestingly, this doesn't seem specific to "tabbing" through elements. From messing around with it for a bit in an emulator, it seems like once I have opened the keyboard once, any keyboard interaction will continue to open it (even when highlighting a button, and not the text field).

So for example, 
1. Click on a TextField (bringing up the keyboard)
2. Dismiss the software-keyboard
3. Click on a button (`OutlinedButton`, in this case).
4. Press 'a' on laptop keyboard.

Results in the keyboard being pulled up. Clicking `a` before ever clicking on the TextField (or otherwise bringing up the software keyboard) doesn't result in it being pulled up, though. But it does print the following to the logs, suggesting perhaps that it is still trying to, but blocked by some permissions?

```
2023-10-30 15:08:36.033   584-1820  InputDispatcher         system_server                        D  Touch mode switch rejected, caller (pid=0, uid=10170) doesn't own the focused window nor none of the previously interacted window
2023-10-30 15:08:36.047  1526-1526  GoogleInpu...hodService com...gle.android.inputmethod.latin  I  GoogleInputMethodService.onFinishInput():3220 
2023-10-30 15:08:36.048  1526-1526  GoogleInpu...hodService com...gle.android.inputmethod.latin  I  GoogleInputMethodService.updateDeviceLockedStatus():2114 repeatCheckTimes = 0, unlocked = true
2023-10-30 15:08:36.048  1526-1526  GoogleInpu...hodService com...gle.android.inputmethod.latin  I  GoogleInputMethodService.onStartInput():1919 onStartInput(EditorInfo{inputType=0x0(NULL) imeOptions=0x0 privateImeOptions=null actionName=UNSPECIFIED actionLabel=null actionId=0 initialSelStart=-1 initialSelEnd=-1 initialCapsMode=0x0 hintText=null label=null packageName=com.example.keyboard_tab fieldId=1 fieldName=null extras=null}, false)
2023-10-30 15:08:36.048  1526-1526  GoogleInpu...hodService com...gle.android.inputmethod.latin  I  GoogleInputMethodService.updateDeviceLockedStatus():2114 repeatCheckTimes = 2, unlocked = true
```

This is how `View.onFocusChanged()` works in Android (the view should be focusable of course - _any_ focusable view, not just buttons). When focus is gained, it calls `notifyFocusChangeToImeFocusController(true)`. This leads to `InputMethodManager.onViewFocusChangedInternal()`. Providing that the window to which the view is attached has IME focus, it calls `ViewRootImpl.dispatchCheckFocus()`. `ViewRootImpl` places `MSG_CHECK_FOCUS` message in the queue of its handler, and here you are: when eventually this message is handled, the keyboard is shown. As ViewRootImpl is hidden from the SDK, one can't do anything about this. As far as I can judge from the comments in the source code, whoever wrote this had something different in mind and didn't foresee the implications.

> So what is happening is, that you are auto validating the whole form instead of each Text form field.

I'm asking why both ways behave differently? (`autovalidateMode: AutovalidateMode.onUserInteraction` on Form widget and on each `TextFormField`)
Both should work the same right..?

@newtaDev 

No, they won't work the same because, on one hand, you are auto validating the form which means any change that occurs in the form will trigger all the potential errors to appear, and on the other hand you are auto validating each TextForm Field which is what you most likely want to do.

Hi @newtaDev, Thanks for filing the issue. I am able to reproduce the issue, validation is run on all the fields even if they are not interacted. As per the [docs](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/AutovalidateMode.html) >onUserInteraction → const [AutovalidateMode](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/AutovalidateMode.html) > Used to auto-validate [Form](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/Form-class.html) and [FormField](https://api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/FormField-class.html) only after each user interaction.

@justinmc what about a new value `AutovalidateMode.singleOnUserInteractionForm` that will have the same behavior as  `AutovalidateMode.onUserInteraction` in a single textfield, and will validate individual fields in a form?

I don't think I'm confident enough that the current behavior should change.  There might be some users relying on the ability to validate all fields when the form is interacted with.

So I like that your idea allows all options @pedromassango.  Some things to think about though:

  1. What happens if you have conflicting values of autovalidateMode between a Form and a FormField?
  2. FormFields can also contain other FormFields, so what if you put `AutovalidateMode.singleOnUserInteractionForm` on an outer FormField?  Does it behave the same or differently than `AutovalidateMode.onUserInteraction`?
Small sample size, but I asked about this on Twitter and nobody liked the default behavior (all fields validated).  https://twitter.com/justinjmcc/status/1584691790502060032

> * What happens if you have conflicting values of autovalidateMode between a Form and a FormField?

The value from the `FormField` widget should take priority, just to follow the Flutter's inherited widget convention were the innermost takes priority. Though we would have an issue: the current default value is `AutovalidateMode.disabled`, what if the user wants to disable validation for that specific field? We won't be able to determine whether the user wants is as default behavior or not.



> 2\. FormFields can also contain other FormFields, so what if you put `AutovalidateMode.singleOnUserInteractionForm` on an outer FormField?  Does it behave the same or differently than `AutovalidateMode.onUserInteraction`?

I would make them behave the same way. The concept behind `singleOnUserInteractionForm` is for it to be used to validate a set of fields that needs to be validated.

> Small sample size, but I asked about this on Twitter and nobody liked the default behavior (all fields validated). https://twitter.com/justinjmcc/status/1584691790502060032

I wouldn’t expect a different result. None wants their user to see an error message without interacting with a field. There may be some exceptions so it is good to keep this option available.
Hi @pedromassango, any updates on this issue? I came across the same problem with validations and _singleOnUserInteractionForm_ would be really useful in my case.
@justinmc any feedback on my proposal above? We have to agree/decide on witch direction we want to go.

After looking into this, I think let's just change the behavior of AutovalidateMode.onUserInteraction.  It should be straightforward to implement and still make sense in all or most cases of nested Forms and FormFields.  After it's merged, we should keep an eye out in case anyone complains about the original behavior being missing.

I think there's some fundamental mismatch between `validate` being an imperative kind of operation mapped onto Flutter's declarative framework.  The more I think about adding onSingleUserInteraction, the more corner cases I see where things don't make sense anymore.

Maybe in the long term, especially if people do complain about the missing behavior, we could rethink AutovalidateMode altogether.  I think we could get to a fully declarative API if we had some kind of parameter (`ValidationDisplayMode`?) that mapped the state of validity to the state of showing or not showing the error UI.

> After looking into this, I think let's just change the behavior of AutovalidateMode.onUserInteraction. It should be straightforward to implement and still make sense in all or most cases of nested Forms and FormFields. After it's merged, we should keep an eye out in case anyone complains about the original behavior being missing.

issues.title: How to share focus with popovers and other sub-tasks
issues.body:
There's a conflict in text-based focus behavior in Flutter and I'm wondering if there's an intended approach to this problem.

Typically, when entering text into something like a form field, the moment focus moves away from the field, the selection is gone. For example, you'll see a caret and type text into a "name" field. Then, focus moves to an "email" field. At this point, there is no caret or selection in the "name" field any longer, and there is a caret in the "email" field. However, this focus behavior is not universal.

When composing text, there are a number of occasions in which some kind of popover might appear. For example, a popover to select text styles, enter a URL for a link, or select a compound character. In these situations, the popover includes focusable elements, and the user should be able to move focus around those elements. But that focus change should **not** impact the selection within the text field. Additionally, the moment the popover disappears, focus should return to the text field with the selection unchanged.

Does Flutter have an approach for handling these different situations?

Visual examples:
* Editing the link URL in a selection toolbar (this example use `SuperEditor` but without keyboard as the input source, not IME)
![Screenshot 2022-06-28 at 23 46 13](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/31278849/176316856-c22ce214-d6d7-4cd6-980e-bffd6b6dedc4.png)

* Right-click on selected to show the context menu
![Screenshot 2022-06-28 at 23 49 08](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/31278849/176317147-c8422558-0810-4a35-973d-5053affa5012.png)

* Press and hold character key that can be written with different accents depending on the language (e.g. Portuguese)
![Screenshot 2022-06-28 at 23 50 53](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/31278849/176317259-b843e0b1-4f26-45eb-b663-51cbda418862.png)
comments: CC @justinmc
Hello @matthew-carroll. Thank you for filing this issue. Can you include the output of `flutter doctor -v` as well as a minimal, reproducible example along with reproduction steps?

The Flutter version doesn't matter for this. It's a question about the approach. All necessary information is in the ticket. I'd like to hear the perspective from someone working on text input, e.g., @justinmc, @gspencergoog, @LongCatIsLooong

Is this related to this problem with FocusTrap? https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/86972

Taps outside of an input will always steal focus from that input because of FocusTrap.

That issue may or may not be related. But the root question here is how a Flutter developer should know when losing focus means "your input isn't active anymore" vs situations that mean "your input is still active, but something else has focus temporarily". You can see those examples in the original post.

>  the moment focus moves away from the field, the selection is gone

`TextField` is considered focused as long as a descendant in its focus tree is focused, even if the `TextField` is not the primary focus, because it mostly relies on [`hasFocus`](https://master-api.flutter.dev/flutter/widgets/FocusNode/hasFocus.html) to determine if it's receiving input.

> When composing text, there are a number of occasions in which some kind of popover might appear. 

If elements in the popover are themselves focusable and have to use dedicated `FocusNode`s, then in theory as long as these `FocusNode`s are descendants of the `FocusNode` you gave to the `TextField`, `TextField` should still consider itself as focused and appear as focused. This may require the popover implementation to find the focus node that belongs to the text field, if the popover is implemented as a separate `OverlayEntry`, since you can't rely on the widget tree hierarchy to automatically do that for you.

It sounds like you're saying that `FocusNode` hierarchy might be different from `Widget` hierarchy. Am I understanding you correctly?

If so, don't `Focus` widgets automatically reparent `FocusNode`s such that a `FocusNode` in an overlay can't be a descendent of a `FocusNode` in the regular tree?

Furthermore, what's the story around platform interop? You'll see in the original post that there are multiple situations where the popover is a system UI, e.g., a context menu or a compound character selection.

That's right. I think the `Focus` widget uses `Focus.of`  to build the focus tree. So if you want the text field to stay focused when something in the popover gains focus, right now you'll probably have to manage the `FocusNode` subtree yourself.

> Furthermore, what's the story around platform interop? You'll see in the original post that there are multiple situations where the popover is a system UI, e.g., a context menu or a compound character selection.

As far as the text input plugin goes I think the flutter framework handles focus separately from the platform's focus system. For key input if the system decides to hand a key event to the FlutterView then the embedder sends the key event to the framework.

Can we get an official Flutter team example of each situation, so that everyone is clear on the intended approach? E.g., a case where focus moves to an in-app popover and back, and a case where focus moves to an OS popover and back?

I'm also wondering if Flutter should offer a generalized capability to achieve this `FocusNode` hierarchy. We can handle a custom hierarchy when the same developer is developing the document editor and the popovers. But consider that super_editor is developing the document editor and app developers are creating any number of popovers. How do we deal with open-ended possibilities for this use-case?

I'll add another detail to this issue. I started to implement custom management of a `FocusNode`'s parent. However, my custom parenting wasn't being respected. The parent-child relationship kept getting changed by something else.

I discovered that `TextField` passes its `FocusNode` to `EditableText`, which then includes a `Focus` widget within itself. Therefore, it looks like `TextField` is forcing reparenting based on the widget tree, preventing me from solving this problem. Is there an intended workaround for this situation?

@matthew-carroll I've assigned labels to this issue. I'm not every familiar with the internals of `TextField` so I am not able to follow the discussion well, but please do confirm if the labels are correct

@exaby73 I'm not sure if the labels are correct or not. I don't know if this is a bug or a new feature. And I don't know if I'm proposing something new, or if there's an intended approach here. That's why I asked the questions above. We need additional input from the Flutter team in multiple areas.

Two ideas based on what I'm seeing on my end:

- Add a `bool` to `Focus` that enables/disables re-parenting altogether. That way, clients can use a `Focus` widget exclusively to respond to key presses without breaking a focus tree structure that was configured elsewhere.
- Add an optional `parentNode` to `Focus`. When provided, the `Focus` widget will always reparent to the `parentNode`, rather than re-parent based on the widget tree structure.

CC @gspencergoog

> Does Flutter have an approach for handling these different situations?

In short, no.

There's no concept of multiple focus, or a "focus stack" other than what happens when you enter and leave a focus scope. You might be able to get something like the functionality you want by wrapping the contents of the overlay in a `FocusScope` and moving the focus there when it pops up (probably via autofocus), and then focusing the text field's `FocusScopeNode` when the overlay goes away. This would not, however, continue to show the focus highlight in the underlying field, it would just return the focus to the right place when the overlay went away.

> It sounds like you're saying that FocusNode hierarchy might be different from Widget hierarchy.

Yes, the focus tree is, in fact, separate from the widget tree and doesn't have to mirror it (even though 99% of the time it does), but in general, it's a bad idea to manipulate `FocusNode` hierarchies outside of the `Focus` and `FocusScope` widgets, since they can reset any attributes of the focus node that they manage, including reparenting them. You would need to manage your own `FocusAttachment`, which the `Focus` widget normally does for you. `EditableText` used to be the only instance in the framework of this, but it now uses a `Focus` widget like everything else.

The existence of public API on `FocusNode` other than `requestFocus` is purely because we didn't want to break old apps that might be using those APIs at the time we did the refactor (probably four years ago now). At this point, they should probably be deprecated. Adding more API to manipulate the focus tree manually is going in the wrong direction, IMO, and complicating an API that is already too complicated.

If what you'd like to do is respond to keypresses regardless of focus, you can add a handler to `HardwareKeyboard`, but I suspect that isn't sufficient.

Some concept of a "focus stack" where there is a stack of currently focused nodes instead of a single primary focus is probably what you need, and the overlain dialogs could push and pop the focus to keep the focus visible below them. This would be non-trivial to implement in a non-breaking way.

@gspencergoog can you take a look at what we're doing in SuperEditor related to this problem?
https://github.com/superlistapp/super_editor/pull/671

I'm not sure I see how creating more focus scopes would solve the problem.

You mentioned that you want to get rid of the properties on `FocusNode`. I believe that would make our use-case impossible.

I think this SuperEditor use-case is likely to proliferate on desktop where we deal with many more intricate popover use-cases. Based on your description of the current state and intentions, it sounds like the focus system might be moving in the opposite direction from where it needs to go.

Or, to state this problem another way: In a world full of popovers, focus structure does NOT match the widget tree structure.

> I'm not sure I see how creating more focus scopes would solve the problem.

I'm sure it won't solve the problem. It would solve part of the problem, which is preserving the correct focus when the popover goes away, since the `FocusScopeNode` does keep track of a stack of previously focused nodes. There's still a lot left that it wouldn't solve.

> Based on your description of the current state and intentions, it sounds like the focus system might be moving in the opposite direction from where it needs to go.

There currently aren't any plans to change the focus system, so it's not really moving in any direction.

> Or, to state this problem another way: In a world full of popovers, focus structure does NOT match the widget tree structure.

I'm not sure that having separate structures necessarily follows from having popovers. There are plenty of designs that would allow the two trees to continue have similar structure, which has the distinct advantage that most developers never need to know that the focus tree could have a different structure.

> I'm not sure that having separate structures necessarily follows from having popovers. There are plenty of designs that would allow the two trees to continue have similar structure, which has the distinct advantage that most developers never need to know that the focus tree could have a different structure.

Can you describe this with some specifics? The fact that there are 2 trees would seem to suggest that the focus structure is incompatible with the tree structure. For example, the `Focus` widget in the `Overlay` walks up the widget tree to attach a parent `FocusNode`. That `Overlay` branch that it's climbing is necessarily separate and independent from the primary UI branch that displays the screen/body. How would one treat these independent trees as a "similar structure" for focus?

I ended up merging in my linked changes into super_editor: https://github.com/superlistapp/super_editor/pull/671

I included a smoke test for the behavior in questions: https://github.com/superlistapp/super_editor/pull/671/files#diff-e291e61fe66a09ed12351d0e94c6c4a789fa6ab6b4ce101fd460b5fffcded826 - hopefully these tests will be added to the test registry soon.

We've ended up with two regrettable changes. First, we had to create a widget that parents a `FocusNode` to a specified parent, instead of the natural upstream parent. Second, and more significantly, we had to copy all of `Focus` and add adjustments to prevent automatic reparenting: https://github.com/superlistapp/super_editor/pull/671/files#diff-474c1650d6279380617b75f98826dc9be6ef1287b2ce87749421ba23ef7d3bf9

If Flutter thinks that it makes this use-case possible with existing tooling, I'd like to see a specific example. If such an example can be assembled, then I would recommend placing it in the framework tests to ensure that the use-case remains functional.

We've run into another issue with sharing focus. As I mentioned in the previous comment, we were able to work around the focus problem with widgets that we fully control. However, widgets like `DropdownButton` specifically require items of type `DropdownMenuItem`, which seem to enforce the standard `Focus` reparenting. I haven't found a place to stop that behavior, and so now I'm not sure how we're supposed to share focus between our toolbar dropdown and the editor.

![Screen Shot 2022-07-13 at 3 05 09 PM](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/7259036/178845320-f50c6477-fd2f-4e71-b974-a6b47542bb13.png)

> just an optional argument to `Focus` itself

This could be very nice :)
I've created a PR to allow setting of the parent focus node explicitly, in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/113655 . Let me know if that addresses your use case.
This issue is missing a priority label. Please set a priority label when adding the `triaged-framework` label.

I would expect an assertion like the one we are discussing adding in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/pull/141671

Having a sliver of infinite height in the group, with another sliver expected to follow (containing the text field here), is not valid. You would never be able to scroll to the second sliver in the group. This is why I think we should assert against this.

I haven't reproduced the crash on Redmi Note 9 Pro (android-arm64 Android 10 (API 29))
So may somebody from flutter team check that issue?

Additionally, [there’s a check using assert](https://github.com/yiiim/flutter/blob/1de678f1bdb17059692786bdc9d0db58e254f59f/packages/flutter/lib/src/rendering/viewport.dart#L1508). Are any additional `assert`-s really necessary?
If no additional `assert`-s  are needed and the bug cannot be reproduced, is it correct to close the issue?
